explicit_text: I was actually born in the St. Andrew's Hotel in New York City on January 30, 1908. The hotel was located at the northwest corner of Broadway and Seventy-second Street, but it was torn down sometime in the twenties or thirties. My mother was staying there to give birth to me since she was originally from New York. It was quite common back then for children to be born at home or in places like hotels, not hospitals.

explicit_text: New York City was where I was born, specifically in the St. Andrew's Hotel. My mother was from New York originally, and my father spent a lot of time there because he helped start the Cone Export and Commission Company's office around 1890. Both my older brothers were also born in New York. My parents met in New York before courting at the World's Columbian Exposition in Chicago.

explicit_text: My brother Ben was born in the summertime, and since our family doctor had a summer place in the Catskill Mountains, my mother rented a house there that summer for his birth. This was back in August, and it was common at the time for children to be born at home rather than hospitals. The Catskill Mountains were a peaceful retreat for my family during that period.

explicit_text: My parents courted quite a bit at the World's Columbian Exposition in Chicago around 1892 or 1893 before they got married. Both their families took trips to this fair, which was a big event at the time. It was a significant moment in their relationship and family history.

explicit_text: My family moved to Greensboro in 1895, about thirteen years before I was born. I grew up there on Summit Avenue, which was outside the city limits at the time. Greensboro was also where my parents bought the Carolina Steel and Iron Company property and built their manufacturing plant and home. I was educated there and later became involved in various civic roles in Greensboro.

I was educated there and later became involved in various civic roles in that city.
cues: Carolina Steel and Iron Company property

explicit_text: My parents bought the defunct Carolina Steel and Iron Company property northeast of Greensboro, which was originally organized around 1880 to make steel. The company had failed, but my family used the land to build a manufacturing plant and their home. This property was essentially a farm where I grew up, complete with cows, chickens, horses, and a silo.

explicit_text: Greensboro was once envisioned as the southern Pittsburgh because of the steel industry ambitions, including the Carolina Steel and Iron Company. However, that steel company went bust, and the vision didn't fully materialize. My family’s involvement with the steel company property was part of that early industrial dream.

explicit_text: For my last two years of high school, I attended Oak Ridge Military Academy after completing two years of high school in Greensboro. This was part of my early education before I went on to the University of North Carolina and later Harvard Business School. Oak Ridge helped prepare me for college and beyond.

explicit_text: I graduated from the University of North Carolina in 1928, where I took many courses in history and economics. My older brothers also attended UNC, though they did not complete full degrees. UNC was an important part of my education before I went on to Harvard Business School.

explicit_text: I attended Harvard Business School after the University of North Carolina, although it wasn't my choice; my family decided it for me after my father died when I was nine. The case method there taught me to reason through problems rather than just memorize answers, which was very helpful in my business career. I found that ordinary intelligence and practical reasoning mattered more than just academic brilliance.

explicit_text: My brother Ben went to school with the famous Thomas Wolfe at the University of North Carolina. Although I didn't attend school with him, Thomas Wolfe was a notable figure connected to my family through my brother's education. It was interesting to have such a literary figure in our circle.

explicit_text: Our company was listed on the New York Stock Exchange in 1951, which marked a turning point in how we ran the business. I was Treasurer at that time, and my oldest brother was President. Going public meant we had to move away from nepotism and start recruiting professional management, which was a significant change for our family company.

explicit_text: I served as President of the Moses H. Cone Memorial Hospital in Greensboro for a while. It was one of the many civic roles I took on beyond my business career. Although I was involved in its leadership, I have been critical of how such institutions are run today, especially regarding spending and borrowing.

explicit_text: Since the time of Franklin D. Roosevelt, I have observed a shift in how money is spent on projects, whether business or civic. Roosevelt's era introduced the idea of throwing more money at problems to solve them, which I believe has led to excessive borrowing and spending. I think this approach burdens future generations with debt and interest.

That president's era introduced the idea of throwing more money at problems to solve them, which I believe has led to excessive borrowing and spending. I think this approach burdens future generations with debt and interest.

explicit_text: The New York City Subway system was built in a time without inflation, and people today benefit from that infrastructure which would cost billions to build now. I think many don't realize how valuable those early investments were. However, I worry about the future generations who will have to bear the costs of maintaining and expanding such systems.
